parentQuestion: A patient reports food has lost its taste, has a dry eye, and cannot close their eye on one side. Which nerve is damaged?

scaffolds.q: Which nerve carries taste sensation from the FRONT 2/3 of the tongue?
scaffolds.explain: CN VII (facial nerve) carries TASTE from the anterior 2/3 of the tongue. If CN VII is damaged, most of the tongue loses taste sensation.

wrongExplains.0: CN V carries TOUCH sensation from the tongue, not taste. Taste and touch are different sensory modalities.
wrongExplains.2: CN IX carries taste from the POSTERIOR 1/3 (back) of the tongue. The front 2/3 uses CN VII.
wrongExplains.3: CN XII controls tongue MOVEMENT, not taste. It's purely motor.

scaffolds.q: Which nerve controls the lacrimal gland that produces tears?
scaffolds.explain: CN VII controls the LACRIMAL GLAND through parasympathetic fibers. If CN VII is damaged, the gland can't produce enough tears, causing dry eye.

wrongExplains.0: CN II carries vision. It doesn't control any glands.
wrongExplains.1: CN III controls pupil and some eye muscles, but not the tear gland. That's CN VII.
wrongExplains.3: CN VIII handles hearing and balance. It has nothing to do with tear production.

scaffolds.q: Which muscle closes the eye, and which nerve controls it?
scaffolds.explain: The ORBICULARIS OCULI muscle circles the eye and squeezes it SHUT. It's controlled by CN VII (facial). CN VII damage = can't close the eye.

wrongExplains.0: The levator palpebrae OPENS the eye (CN III). The orbicularis oculi CLOSES it (CN VII). Opposite actions.
wrongExplains.2: The lateral rectus moves the eyeball sideways (CN VI). It doesn't close the eyelid.
wrongExplains.3: The superior oblique rotates the eye downward (CN IV). It doesn't affect the eyelid.

scaffolds.q: A patient has THREE symptoms: Lost taste, dry eye, and can't close eye. What do these have in common?
scaffolds.explain: ALL THREE are CN VII functions: Taste (anterior tongue), tear production (lacrimal gland), and eye closure (orbicularis oculi). One nerve damage = all three symptoms.

wrongExplains.0: CN V handles facial SENSATION and CHEWING. It doesn't control taste, tears, or eye closure.
wrongExplains.2: They're not unrelated — they all use CN VII. This is why they appear together.
wrongExplains.3: CN X handles voice, swallowing, and internal organs. These eye/taste symptoms are CN VII.

scaffolds.q: What condition commonly causes sudden CN VII damage with these symptoms?
scaffolds.explain: BELL'S PALSY is sudden, usually temporary facial nerve paralysis. It causes facial weakness, dry eye, taste changes, and inability to close the eye on one side.

wrongExplains.0: Heart attack affects the heart (blood supply), not the facial nerve. Different problem entirely.
wrongExplains.2: A broken leg involves bones and different nerves. It doesn't affect CN VII in the face.
wrongExplains.3: Stomach flu affects the GI tract. Bell's palsy specifically affects the facial nerve.

scaffolds.q: Why is a dry eye dangerous in CN VII palsy?
options: The cornea can dry out and become damaged without tears
scaffolds.explain: Without tears, the CORNEA dries out and can get scratched or infected (keratitis). Plus, the eye can't close to protect itself. Patients need artificial tears and eye protection.

wrongExplains.0: Dry eye IS dangerous. Without tears and eye closure, the cornea can be permanently damaged.
wrongExplains.2: Deafness involves CN VIII, not CN VII. Dry eye doesn't cause hearing problems.
wrongExplains.3: It doesn't cause immediate blindness, but prolonged corneal damage CAN eventually impair vision.

scaffolds.q: CN VII damage causes facial weakness. Why can the patient still FEEL their face?
options: Facial SENSATION uses CN V, which is intact
Feeling and movement use the same nerve
The patient is imagining the sensation
scaffolds.explain: CN V handles facial SENSATION. CN VII handles facial MOVEMENT. They're different nerves! So CN VII damage causes paralysis but NOT numbness. The patient can feel but can't move.

wrongExplains.0: CN VII is primarily motor (plus taste and glands). Facial touch sensation uses CN V.
wrongExplains.2: Feeling (CN V) and movement (CN VII) use DIFFERENT nerves. That's why one can fail while the other works.
wrongExplains.3: The sensation is real — CN V is working normally, transmitting touch information to the brain.

scaffolds.q: What treatment might a CN VII palsy patient need for eye protection?
options: Artificial tears and eye taping at night
scaffolds.explain: ARTIFICIAL TEARS replace missing tear production. EYE TAPING at night keeps the eye closed during sleep since the patient can't close it voluntarily. This prevents corneal damage.

wrongExplains.0: Hearing aids are for hearing loss (CN VIII). CN VII palsy doesn't affect hearing.
wrongExplains.2: Glasses correct vision problems. The issue here is eye protection, not visual acuity.
wrongExplains.3: Chewing uses CN V. The eye protection problem relates to CN VII controlling tears and eye closure.

scaffolds.q: The patient notices the affected side of their face droops and they can't raise that eyebrow. This confirms damage to which nerve?
scaffolds.explain: Facial drooping and inability to raise the eyebrow = facial EXPRESSION muscle paralysis = CN VII damage. All muscles of facial expression are controlled by CN VII.

wrongExplains.0: CN V provides facial SENSATION and controls CHEWING. It doesn't move the eyebrow or control facial expression.
wrongExplains.2: CN III controls eye movement and eyelid LIFTING. Eyebrow raising is a facial expression controlled by CN VII.
wrongExplains.3: CN XII controls TONGUE movement. It doesn't affect facial expression or eyebrow movement.
